title: Inside the Pool of Gibeon: 3D Model Reconstruction and Some Observations about Finds
author: Maria Tamburrini
allowedUseNotes: Author-copyright preprint. Project stores its own summary of the dating argument only; no reproduction of the 3D model, drawings, or finds photography.
summary: A reassessment of Pritchard's Gibeon (el-Jib) excavation records and finds, produced during Tamburrini's 2019 Sapienza University of Rome PhD dissertation on Iron I Palestine (a critical re-examination of settlement, population, and material culture using the original excavation data). Revisits the stratigraphy and fill of the site's rock-cut 'cylindrical pool' (the feature identified with 'the pool of Gibeon' named in 2 Samuel 2:13) using a 3D model reconstruction. Reported finding, per secondary summaries of the paper (see confidenceNotes): the pool's own cutting/construction is dated to the 10th century BCE, distinct from a separate, later cluster of artifacts recovered from the same fill dated to the 8th-6th centuries BCE, which the author reads as possibly reflecting more than one depositional episode rather than a single continuous accumulation. This distinguishes the pool's own excavated construction date from the well-known later inscribed jar-handle debris in its fill (see pritchard-gibeon-1962), and is the closest thing this project has found to a direct scholarly answer to whether the pool predates, is contemporary with, or postdates the early Iron IIA setting of 2 Samuel 2: on this reassessment, a 10th-century-BCE cutting is at or very close to the conventional early United Monarchy horizon, not a later Iron IIB/C construction.
confidenceNotes: TO VERIFY: this project could not directly read the preprint itself (academia.edu and researchgate listing pages both returned HTTP 403 to this project's outbound fetch tool during this research pass); the summary above is reconstructed from multiple independent web-search result snippets describing the paper's abstract/findings, cross-checked against each other for consistency, not from a full read of the argument or its stratigraphic evidence. The preprint's peer-review/final-publication status is unclear from what could be found (the DOI resolves to an OSF preprint server, not a journal issue); treat the '10th century BCE' figure as a reported claim from a specialist (Tamburrini's doctoral research is specifically on Iron I Palestine material culture) rather than an established consensus date. Also TO VERIFY: exact page/section citation, and reconciliation with the differing '11th century BCE' and 'late 12th/early 13th century BCE' dates for the same pool reported elsewhere in secondary literature attributed to Pritchard's own original excavation conclusions (see pritchard-gibeon-1962's updated confidenceNotes) — the two datings differ by two to three centuries but both fall at or before the early Iron IIA setting of 2 Samuel 2, which is the specific question this project needed answered.
